header.title: Mental Capacity Act — principles, assessment, best interest decisions

body.summary: The Mental Capacity Act protects people's right to make their own decisions — including decisions you might disagree with.
body.text:
TOPIC: Mental Capacity Act — principles and best interests
DURATION TARGET: 4–5 minutes
ELEVENLABS VOICE SUGGESTION: Speaker A — "Aria" (warm female), Speaker B — "Marcus" (calm, reassuring male)

[SPEAKER A]: Can I run something past you? Mr Okafor has a hospital appointment tomorrow, a check on his heart, and he's flat out refusing to go. I'm worried about him. Should I just book the transport anyway and get him there?

[SPEAKER B]: First of all, I'm glad you came to me. But no, you can't make him go, and you shouldn't try to. The starting point is that we assume every adult can make their own decisions. We only treat someone as unable to decide once that's actually been proven, with a proper assessment.

[SPEAKER A]: But not going feels like a bad decision. It could hurt him.

[SPEAKER B]: It might be. But here's the bit people get wrong. A person has the right to make a choice that you and I would call unwise or even eccentric. Making a decision you disagree with does not mean they've lost capacity. We all have our own values and reasons. You can't say someone lacks capacity just because their choice worries you.

[SPEAKER A]: So I just leave it? That feels like giving up on him.

[SPEAKER B]: Not at all. Before we ever decide someone can't choose, we have to support them to choose. Have you sat with him and found out why? Maybe he's frightened, maybe he had a bad experience last time, maybe he hasn't understood what the appointment is for.

[SPEAKER A]: I hadn't really asked properly.

[SPEAKER B]: That's the work. Give him the information in a way he can take in. Pick a good time of day, somewhere calm. Use simple words, pictures if they help. Check he can hear you, see you. Ask if he'd like a relative there. You'd be surprised how often a refusal turns into a yes once the person actually understands what's happening.

[SPEAKER A]: And if I do all that and he still says no?

[SPEAKER B]: Then that's his decision to make, and we respect it. But if you genuinely doubt whether he can make this particular decision, that's when we do a capacity assessment. And it's specific. We're not asking "can Mr Okafor make decisions." We're asking, can he make this one, about this appointment, right now? Capacity is decision by decision, and it can change day to day.

[SPEAKER A]: How do you actually test that?

[SPEAKER B]: There's a simple way to think about it. Can he understand the information about the choice? Can he hold onto it long enough to use it? Can he weigh it up, the pros and the cons? And can he communicate his decision back to you, by any means at all? If he can't do one or more of those, because of something affecting his mind or brain, then he may lack capacity for this decision. And we write down exactly how we worked that out.

[SPEAKER A]: Say he does lack capacity. Then what?

[SPEAKER B]: Then, and only then, we make a decision in his best interests. That doesn't mean we decide for him in a back room. He stays at the centre. We look at his past wishes, his feelings, his beliefs. We talk to his family, his GP, anyone who knows him. And we always pick the option that limits his freedom the least while still keeping him safe.

[SPEAKER A]: What if he's got no family or friends to ask? It's just us, the paid staff.

[SPEAKER B]: Good question, and an important one. If it's a serious decision, like major medical treatment or a move, and there's nobody independent to speak for him, then an independent advocate, an IMCA, has to be brought in. They're appointed for him, they find out what matters to him, and they make sure his voice is in the room. You'd flag that to me and I'd sort it.

[SPEAKER A]: That makes it much clearer. So my job tomorrow is to talk to him properly, not to fight him.

[SPEAKER B]: Exactly. Support first. And come straight back to me if you're unsure.

---
KEY TAKEAWAYS (for show notes / text card pairing):
1. Assume capacity and support the person to decide first — an unwise or risky choice is not proof that someone lacks capacity.
2. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. Assess whether the person can understand, retain, weigh and communicate this decision, and write down how you decided.
3. Only if capacity is lacking do you make a best-interests decision — involve the person, family and professionals, choose the least restrictive option, and bring in an IMCA when there's no family or friend to consult.
